title: FOMC Statement November 2010: Additional Asset Purchases to Support Recovery
body: To promote a stronger pace of economic recovery and to help ensure that inflation, over time, is at levels consistent with its mandate, the Committee decided today to expand its holdings of securities. The Committee will maintain its existing policy of reinvesting principal payments from its securities holdings. In addition, the Committee intends to purchase a further $600 billion of longer-term Treasury securities by the end of the second quarter of 2011, a pace of about $75 billion per month. The Committee will regularly review the pace of its securities purchases and the overall size of the asset-purchase program in light of incoming information and will adjust the program as needed to best foster maximum employment and price stability. The federal funds rate has been held at its effective lower bound, and the Committee anticipates that economic conditions are likely to warrant exceptionally low levels for the federal funds rate for an extended period. Large-scale asset purchases represent an unconventional tool to provide additional monetary accommodation when the policy rate is constrained by the zero lower bound.

title: Bernanke Jackson Hole 2012: Monetary Policy Since the Onset of the Crisis
body: The Federal Reserve's response to the financial crisis and ensuing recession has involved several components. Most significantly, the FOMC cut its target for the federal funds rate nearly to zero by the end of 2008, where it has remained since. With the federal funds rate at its effective lower bound, the Federal Reserve has also employed unconventional tools to provide additional accommodation. The second major component of the Federal Reserve's response is forward guidance about the likely future path of the federal funds rate. The third component is large-scale asset purchases. The total face value of agency securities, agency mortgage-backed securities, and longer-term Treasury securities held in the Federal Reserve's portfolio has increased from around $500 billion before the crisis to approximately $2.8 trillion currently. The Federal Reserve's asset purchases have eased financial conditions and provided meaningful support to the recovery. The costs of nontraditional policies must be weighed against the potential benefits.

title: Bernanke Congressional Testimony May 2013: Taper of Asset Purchases in Coming Months
body: The Committee has emphasized that the pace of asset purchases is not on a preset course and will respond to changes in the economic outlook as well as to improvements in the labor market and in the inflation outlook. If the incoming data are broadly consistent with this forecast, the Committee currently anticipates that it would be appropriate to moderate the monthly pace of purchases later this year. Asset prices have risen across several categories as investors have moved into higher-yielding securities, raising questions about reach-for-yield behavior and the potential for financial instability. Long-term Treasury yields moved sharply higher following the testimony as market participants reassessed the expected path of the federal funds rate and the duration of large-scale asset purchases. The Committee is aware that a too-rapid withdrawal of accommodation could create conditions that would slow the recovery.

title: FOMC December 2012: Threshold-Based Forward Guidance on Rate Liftoff
body: The Committee decided to keep the target range for the federal funds rate at 0 to 1/4 percent and currently anticipates that this exceptionally low range for the federal funds rate will be appropriate at least as long as the unemployment rate remains above 6-1/2 percent, inflation between one and two years ahead is projected to be no more than a half percentage point above the Committee's 2 percent longer-run goal, and longer-term inflation expectations continue to be well anchored. This guidance represents a significant evolution from the date-based forward guidance previously employed. By specifying economic conditions that must be satisfied before liftoff, the Committee is providing more transparency about the factors that will guide its future policy decisions. The Committee also decided to continue purchasing agency mortgage-backed securities at a pace of $40 billion per month and to purchase longer-term Treasury securities after its program to extend the average maturity of its holdings is completed at the end of the year, initially at a pace of $45 billion per month.

title: IMF World Economic Outlook October 2012: Coping with High Debt and Sluggish Growth
body: Global growth is sluggish and risks remain elevated. A key issue remains the adjustment of the major economies and the risks of excessive fiscal consolidation that could undermine recovery in the short term. In the United States, a major fiscal cliff consisting of automatic tax increases and spending cuts set to take effect in early 2013 creates significant downside risk. In the euro area, the sovereign debt crisis continues to weigh on financial conditions and confidence. Advanced economies face the challenge of achieving fiscal consolidation without undermining near-term growth, while emerging economies must manage the risks from highly accommodative global financial conditions and capital flow volatility. Global growth is projected at 3.3 percent in 2012, below the 3.5 percent forecast in the April 2012 WEO. The risks to the forecast are to the downside, with a probability of global growth falling below 2 percent estimated at around 17 percent.

title: BIS Quarterly Review December 2013: Tapering Talk and Emerging Market Volatility
body: Financial markets experienced significant turbulence following signals that the Federal Reserve might begin tapering its large-scale asset purchase program sooner than anticipated. Emerging market currencies and bond yields came under pressure as investors reassessed the global interest rate environment. Capital flows reversed sharply in several emerging economies that had benefited from the search for yield during the period of ultra-low interest rates in advanced economies. The taper tantrum illustrated the interconnectedness of global financial conditions and the sensitivity of markets to changes in the stance of the Federal Reserve. Credit conditions tightened in several emerging markets as external financing conditions worsened. Bank leverage ratios remain elevated in some jurisdictions, and supervisors have emphasized the importance of building capital buffers during the current period of low volatility.

title: Beige Book November 2011: Housing Remains Depressed, Consumer Spending Mixed
body: Reports from the twelve Federal Reserve Districts indicated that economic activity generally continued to expand at a modest pace. Consumer spending was mixed, with retail sales showing modest growth in some Districts but declining in others, as high gasoline prices and elevated unemployment weighed on household budgets. Manufacturing activity generally continued to grow, though at a slower pace than earlier in the year. Residential real estate conditions remained depressed in most Districts, with home prices continuing to decline in many areas and the number of properties in foreclosure or approaching foreclosure remaining elevated. Demand for home purchase mortgage loans was weak, though refinancing activity picked up as interest rates fell to record lows. Commercial real estate conditions were similarly weak, with vacancy rates high and rents still declining in many markets. Employment conditions improved modestly, though hiring remained cautious.

title: Bernanke on Fiscal Sequestration: Short-Term Headwinds and Long-Run Consolidation
body: The automatic spending cuts scheduled to take effect in March 2013 under the sequestration provisions will likely reduce real GDP growth by about 0.6 percentage point during 2013. This near-term fiscal restraint adds to significant fiscal headwinds already present. The appropriate trajectory for fiscal policy involves near-term flexibility while establishing credible plans for medium- and long-term debt sustainability. High debt-to-GDP ratios in advanced economies create risks for future growth if not addressed through a combination of revenue and spending adjustments. At the same time, excessive austerity in the near term when private demand is still recovering and monetary policy is constrained by the zero lower bound can prove self-defeating. The relationship between fiscal consolidation and growth depends critically on the composition of adjustments and the cyclical position of the economy.

title: FOMC December 2015: Liftoff from Zero After Seven Years
body: The Committee decided to raise the target range for the federal funds rate to 1/4 to 1/2 percent. The stance of monetary policy remains accommodative after this increase, thereby supporting further improvement in labor market conditions and a return to 2 percent inflation. The Committee expects that economic conditions will evolve in a manner that will warrant only gradual increases in the federal funds rate. The actual path of the federal funds rate will depend on the economic outlook as informed by incoming data. The decision to begin policy normalization at this meeting is supported by considerable improvement in labor market conditions during the year. The unemployment rate declined from 5.7 percent in January 2015 to 5.0 percent in October. Core PCE price inflation remained below the Committee's 2 percent objective, held down in part by earlier declines in energy prices and the effects of past dollar appreciation on import prices.

title: Yellen Jackson Hole 2016: The Federal Reserve's Monetary Policy Toolkit
body: The case for an increase in the federal funds rate has strengthened in recent months. The labor market has continued to improve, and preliminary gross domestic product data point to improved economic growth. The Federal Open Market Committee continues to anticipate that gradual increases in the federal funds rate will be appropriate as the labor market strengthens further and inflation moves back to 2 percent. In light of the continued solid performance of the labor market and our outlook for economic activity and inflation, I believe the case for an increase in the federal funds rate has strengthened in recent months. The normalization of monetary policy is proceeding cautiously, with the Committee cognizant of the global headwinds created by weak growth abroad and tighter financial conditions. The neutral federal funds rate appears to have declined in recent years, reflecting lower trend growth and other structural factors, which has important implications for the degree of policy accommodation provided at any given level of the federal funds rate.

title: BIS Quarterly Review September 2019: Yield Curve Inversion and Recession Signals
body: The US Treasury yield curve inverted during August 2019, with two-year yields exceeding ten-year yields for the first time since 2007, sparking widespread commentary about the historical reliability of this signal as a leading indicator of recession. The spread between the federal funds rate and long-term Treasury yields turned negative, reflecting market expectations that the Federal Reserve would need to cut rates substantially to forestall a downturn. Corporate credit spreads widened modestly as recession probabilities in investor surveys increased. The term premium embedded in long-term yields declined markedly, reflecting safe-haven demand and the global search for yield against a backdrop of negative-yielding sovereign debt in Europe and Japan. Central banks in several advanced economies were already easing policy, highlighting the synchronized nature of the global slowdown driven by trade uncertainty and weakening industrial production.

title: IMF World Economic Outlook April 2018: Cyclical Upswing, Structural Change
body: The global economy continues to strengthen, with growth momentum broad-based across both advanced and emerging market economies. However, risks are increasingly to the downside. The imposition of tariffs and potential for trade policy escalation represent a significant threat to the synchronized expansion underway. Trade restrictions could disrupt global value chains and reduce growth in both the directly affected economies and their trading partners. Financial vulnerabilities have built up in several jurisdictions, including elevated asset valuations, high corporate indebtedness, and stretched household balance sheets. Rising protectionism could reduce global trade flows and undermine the rules-based multilateral trading system that has supported decades of economic integration. Policymakers should resist protectionist pressures and work together to ensure the expansion is sustained and shared broadly.

title: FOMC July 2019: Mid-Cycle Insurance Cut to Support Expansion
body: The Committee decided to lower the target range for the federal funds rate to 2 to 2-1/4 percent. This action supports the Committee's view that sustained expansion of economic activity, strong labor market conditions, and inflation near the Committee's symmetric 2 percent objective are the most likely outcomes. The projected slowdown in global growth and trade policy uncertainty, along with muted inflation pressures, provided the basis for this adjustment. Some Committee members characterized the action as an insurance cut against downside risks rather than the beginning of a new easing cycle. The labor market has remained strong with a low unemployment rate, but business investment and manufacturing sentiment have softened amid elevated trade uncertainty. The yield curve had flattened substantially over the preceding months, with short-term rates approaching long-term rates, reflecting market expectations for lower rates ahead.

title: Beige Book July 2018: Tariff Uncertainty Weighs on Business Confidence
body: Economic activity continued to expand at a moderate pace across all twelve Federal Reserve Districts. Consumer spending grew modestly, with solid retail sales and brisk auto sales in most Districts. Manufacturing activity generally continued to expand, but contacts in several Districts noted that uncertainty about trade policy and the effect of tariffs on input costs was causing them to delay capital investment decisions. Tariffs on steel and aluminum were cited as increasing costs for manufacturers in several districts, particularly in the Midwest. Agricultural contacts in the Midwest expressed concern about retaliatory tariffs on US exports, particularly soybeans and pork, and their potential impact on farm income. Residential real estate activity showed mixed results, with continued strong demand for housing offset by tight inventory and rising mortgage rates. Commercial real estate conditions remained generally positive.

title: Federal Reserve Financial Stability Report 2018: Elevated Asset Valuations and Leverage
body: Vulnerabilities in the US financial system are at a moderate level overall, though several specific areas warrant attention. Asset valuations across a range of markets appear elevated relative to historical norms, raising the potential for sharp price declines if sentiment changes. Business sector debt has grown faster than GDP, and the share of new leveraged loans with fewer protections for lenders has increased. By contrast, bank capital ratios are at historically high levels and leverage in the household sector has declined since the financial crisis. The amount of short-term funding used by broker-dealers and money market mutual funds has declined, reducing one form of liquidity risk. The financial system's resilience has been significantly enhanced by post-crisis regulatory reforms, including higher capital and liquidity requirements and stress testing, though some risks remain from the continuing low interest rate environment and the compression of credit risk premiums.

title: IMF World Economic Outlook April 2017: Gaining Momentum
body: The global economy is gaining momentum, with growth in both advanced and emerging market economies exceeding earlier projections. However, medium-term prospects remain subdued in many countries, and high debt levels in both the public and private sectors limit the capacity for fiscal stimulus. The prospect of fiscal expansion in the United States, including potential tax cuts and infrastructure spending, has boosted growth expectations, though the composition, magnitude, and timing of such measures remain uncertain. Fiscal policy space varies widely across countries, with some able to ease policy to support growth and others facing the need for continued consolidation to maintain debt sustainability. Rising debt-to-GDP ratios in several advanced economies create risks for long-term growth prospects. Structural reforms that raise productive capacity and labor force participation remain essential for sustaining growth over the medium term.

title: FOMC Emergency Action March 2020: Rate Cut to Zero and QE Amid COVID-19 Pandemic
body: The coronavirus outbreak has harmed communities and disrupted economic activity in many countries, including the United States. Global financial conditions have also been significantly affected. Available economic data show that the US economy came into this challenging period on a strong footing. Information received since the Committee's January meeting indicates that the labor market remained strong through February and economic activity rose at a moderate rate. In light of these developments, the Committee decided to lower the target range for the federal funds rate to 0 to 1/4 percent. The Committee expects to maintain this target range until it is confident that the economy has weathered recent events and is on track to achieve its maximum employment and price stability goals. To support the smooth functioning of markets for Treasury securities and agency mortgage-backed securities that are central to the flow of credit to households and businesses, the Committee will increase its holdings of Treasury securities by at least $500 billion and its holdings of agency mortgage-backed securities by at least $200 billion.

title: IMF World Economic Outlook April 2020: The Great Lockdown
body: The COVID-19 pandemic is inflicting high and rising human costs worldwide, and the necessary protection measures are severely impacting economic activity. As a result of the pandemic, the global economy is projected to contract sharply by 3 percent in 2020, much worse than during the 2008–09 financial crisis. In a baseline scenario which assumes that the pandemic fades in the second half of 2020, global growth is projected to rebound to 5.8 percent in 2021. However, for many countries the recession will be far deeper, and the recovery will be sluggish at best. The crisis calls for decisive, large-scale fiscal support and monetary accommodation. The economic fallout depends on factors that interact in ways that are hard to predict: the pathway of the pandemic, the intensity and efficacy of containment efforts, the extent of supply disruptions, the repercussions of the dramatic tightening in global financial conditions, shifts in expenditure patterns, behavioral changes such as the avoidance of contact-intensive activities, and confidence effects.

title: Powell Jackson Hole 2020: New Average Inflation Targeting Framework
body: The FOMC has approved a revised Statement on Longer-Run Goals and Monetary Policy Strategy that reflects our updated assessment of how best to pursue our mandate. In our new framework, we will seek to achieve inflation that averages 2 percent over time. Following periods when inflation has been running persistently below 2 percent, appropriate monetary policy will likely aim to achieve inflation moderately above 2 percent for some time. This change does not signal that the Committee seeks inflation markedly above 2 percent, nor a permanent shift in the long-run inflation target. The change means the Committee will not tighten monetary policy solely in response to a strong labor market. Our new statement recognizes that maximum employment is a broad-based and inclusive goal and acknowledges the importance of shortfalls of employment from its maximum level. The Federal Reserve will use its full range of tools to achieve maximum employment and price stability.

title: BIS Quarterly Review December 2021: Inflation Risks and the Central Bank Dilemma
body: Inflation in advanced economies has risen sharply in 2021, driven by pandemic-related supply chain disruptions, strong demand for goods, and surging energy prices. The debate over whether this inflation is transitory or more persistent has intensified, with central banks facing difficult communication challenges. Supply bottlenecks remain acute in semiconductors, shipping, and other sectors, with container freight costs at record highs and delivery times at historic lows. The combination of elevated goods demand—boosted by fiscal transfers—and constrained supply has generated price pressures that are broader-based than initially anticipated. Labor markets are tightening rapidly, raising concerns about wage-price dynamics in some sectors. Central banks must navigate between the risk of tightening prematurely and choking the recovery, and the risk of allowing inflation expectations to become unanchored. The neutral rate of interest remains uncertain, complicating assessments of the appropriate policy stance.

title: FOMC November 2021: Beginning Tapering of Asset Purchases
body: Supply and demand imbalances related to the pandemic and the reopening of the economy have continued to contribute to elevated levels of inflation. Job gains have been solid in recent months, and the unemployment rate has declined substantially. Progress on vaccinations and an easing of supply constraints are expected to support continued gains in economic activity and employment as well as a reduction in inflation. In light of the substantial further progress the economy has made toward the Committee's goals since last December, the Committee decided to begin reducing the monthly pace of its net asset purchases by $10 billion for Treasury securities and $5 billion for agency mortgage-backed securities. The Committee judges that similar reductions in the pace of net asset purchases will likely be appropriate each month, but it is prepared to adjust the pace of purchases if warranted by changes in the economic outlook. The Committee's decisions regarding the federal funds rate remain separate from and not directly linked to those regarding the pace of asset purchases.

title: IMF World Economic Outlook April 2022: War Sets Back the Global Recovery
body: The war in Ukraine has dealt a major blow to the global recovery, which was already facing headwinds from pandemic-related supply disruptions and rising inflation. The war will sharply slow growth and add to inflation in 2022. Russia's invasion of Ukraine has triggered a humanitarian crisis, with millions of refugees. The war has disrupted commodity markets, causing energy and food prices to spike further above already elevated levels. Global growth is now projected to slow from an estimated 6.1 percent in 2021 to 3.6 percent in both 2022 and 2023, a markdown of 0.8 and 0.2 percentage points for 2022 and 2023 respectively relative to the January 2022 projections. Inflation has been revised up markedly, and central banks face the difficult task of tightening policy to contain inflation without choking off growth. Stagflation risks are elevated in several European economies heavily dependent on Russian energy.

title: Beige Book March 2022: Broad-Based Price Increases, Supply Chains Still Strained
body: Economic activity expanded at a modest to moderate pace across the twelve Federal Reserve Districts. Price pressures remained elevated and were broad-based across all Districts and sectors. Several Districts noted that firms were successfully passing through cost increases to customers to a greater degree than in the past. Supply chain disruptions persisted in most Districts, with continued shortages of semiconductors, construction materials, and labor. Energy prices rose sharply in the weeks following Russia's invasion of Ukraine, adding to already elevated inflation in transportation, manufacturing, and agriculture. Wage growth was robust and widespread, with employers raising pay to attract and retain workers in a very tight labor market. Many contacts expressed concern that wage increases would contribute to sustained price pressures. Residential real estate demand remained very strong despite rapidly rising home prices, though some Districts noted that affordability constraints were beginning to dampen buyer activity.

title: FOMC July 2022: Second Consecutive 75 Basis Point Hike to Fight Inflation
body: The Committee decided to raise the target range for the federal funds rate to 2-1/4 to 2-1/2 percent and anticipates that ongoing increases in the target range will be appropriate. Recent indicators of spending and production have softened. Nonetheless, job gains have been robust in recent months, and the unemployment rate has remained low. Inflation remains elevated, reflecting supply and demand imbalances related to the pandemic, higher food and energy prices, and broader price pressures. The Committee is highly attentive to inflation risks and remains strongly committed to returning inflation to its 2 percent objective. The pace and ultimate level of rate increases will depend on incoming data. The Committee also decided to continue reducing its holdings of Treasury securities and agency debt and agency mortgage-backed securities, as described in the Plans for Reducing the Size of the Federal Reserve's Balance Sheet published in May 2022. Quantitative tightening is proceeding as planned.

title: Beige Book September 2022: Housing Market Cools Sharply as Mortgage Rates Surge
body: Residential real estate conditions deteriorated markedly across all twelve Federal Reserve Districts. Rapidly rising mortgage rates, which moved above 6 percent for a 30-year fixed-rate loan for the first time since 2008, combined with still-elevated home prices to create significant affordability challenges for prospective buyers. Home sales declined sharply in most Districts, with several noting that the pace of decline exceeded what was seen during the 2008 financial crisis. New home construction slowed as builders faced both demand weakness and continued supply-side challenges including material shortages and elevated construction costs. Commercial real estate conditions were more mixed, with continued strength in industrial and logistics properties but weakness in office space as remote work patterns persisted. Consumer spending showed signs of rotation back toward services as pandemic-era demand for goods softened, and real consumer spending was constrained by still-elevated inflation.

title: Powell Jackson Hole 2022: Restoring Price Stability at the Cost of Pain
body: Price stability is the responsibility of the Federal Reserve and serves as the bedrock of our economy. Without price stability, the economy does not work for anyone. The FOMC's overarching focus right now is to bring inflation back down to our 2 percent goal. In July, the FOMC raised the policy rate by another 75 basis points; by summer, the policy rate was already above the peak level reached in the 2018–19 cycle. Reducing inflation is likely to require a sustained period of below-trend growth, and there will very likely be some softening of labor market conditions. While higher interest rates, slower growth, and softer labor market conditions will bring down inflation, they will also bring some pain to households and businesses. These are the unfortunate costs of reducing inflation. Restoring price stability will take some time and requires using our tools forcefully to bring demand and supply into better balance. We are moving our policy stance purposefully to a level that will be sufficiently restrictive to return inflation to 2 percent.

title: Powell November 2021 Senate Testimony: Time to Retire Transitory
body: At this point the economy is very strong and inflationary pressures are high, and it is therefore appropriate in my view to consider wrapping up the taper of our asset purchases perhaps a few months sooner. The word transitory has different meanings to different people. It is probably a good time to retire that word and try to explain more clearly what we mean. We use it to mean that it won't leave a permanent mark in the form of higher inflation. Supply-side constraints have proven to be longer-lasting and more widespread than anticipated. Monetary policy decisions are guided by our mandate to promote maximum employment and stable prices for the American people. The factors driving inflation are related to the pandemic and its effect on both supply and demand, and policy cannot do much about supply-side constraints. But if we see signs that inflation expectations are becoming unanchored or that inflationary pressures are becoming broader and more persistent, we will use our tools to maintain price stability.

title: Fed Monetary Policy Report March 2023: SVB Failure, Banking Stress, and Financial Stability
body: The emergence of stress at some U.S. banks and at Credit Suisse in early March 2023 led to sharp declines in bank equity prices and turbulence in short-term funding markets. The failures of Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank were driven by an unusual combination of factors: a concentrated business model, heavy reliance on uninsured deposits, rapid growth funded by long-duration fixed-rate assets, and the pace and magnitude of monetary policy tightening. The Federal Reserve acted swiftly to contain contagion risk, creating the Bank Term Funding Program to provide liquidity to eligible depository institutions. Concerns about deposit flight spread to other regional banks, including First Republic, leading to a broad reassessment of regional bank business models and funding structures. The FOMC remains attentive to the tightening in credit conditions that banking stress may produce, representing an additional source of restraint on economic activity beyond the effects of higher interest rates.

title: BIS Quarterly Review September 2023: Financial Fragility After Banking Stress
body: The banking stress of early 2023 highlighted how the rapid rise in interest rates can expose vulnerabilities in bank balance sheets, particularly for institutions with large portfolios of long-duration securities and high concentrations of uninsured deposits. While the immediate contagion was contained, the episode led to a reassessment of interest rate risk management across the banking sector and prompted regulatory discussions about deposit insurance, capital requirements, and the appropriate scope of stress testing. The AT1 bond writedown in the Credit Suisse resolution raised questions about the hierarchy of creditors in bank resolution and the future pricing of bank subordinated debt. Credit conditions tightened noticeably in the United States in the months following the banking stress, as smaller banks pulled back on lending activity. The interaction between monetary policy tightening and financial system vulnerabilities remains a key risk for the global outlook, with real estate exposures—particularly commercial real estate—representing a significant source of concern for regional lenders.

title: FOMC November 2023: Holding Rates, Higher for Longer Stance
body: The Committee decided to maintain the target range for the federal funds rate at 5-1/4 to 5-1/2 percent. Inflation has moderated from its peak but remains above the Committee's 2 percent longer-run goal. The economy grew at a strong pace in the third quarter, supported by resilient consumer spending. Job gains have moderated since earlier in the year but remain strong. The Committee remains highly attentive to inflation risks and will carefully assess incoming data and the evolving outlook in determining the extent to which additional policy firming may be appropriate. In determining the cumulative tightening of monetary policy, the lags with which monetary policy affects economic activity and inflation, and economic and financial developments, the Committee will continue to take a holistic view of all relevant information. The full effects of the tightening cycle may not yet have been felt, and restrictive policy should continue to work to bring inflation back to target over time.

title: IMF World Economic Outlook October 2023: Navigating Global Divergences
body: Global growth is projected to slow from 3.5 percent in 2022 to 3.0 percent in 2023 and 2.9 percent in 2024, well below the historical average of 3.8 percent. The global economy is limping along. It is not yet in the clear. While a soft landing remains possible for the global economy, downside risks predominate. The necessary tightening of monetary policy in many countries has slowed economic activity, and the lags in the transmission of monetary policy mean that further effects are still to come. Services inflation remains sticky in several economies even as goods inflation has declined. Emerging markets face the challenge of managing capital outflows and currency depreciation amid a strong dollar. China's recovery has disappointed expectations, with weakness in the property sector and subdued consumer confidence creating headwinds. Fiscal policy space has narrowed significantly in most countries, limiting the capacity for countercyclical support if growth disappoints.

title: Fed Monetary Policy Report February 2024: Disinflation Progress and Soft Landing Prospects
body: Inflation has eased substantially from its peak, with total PCE prices rising 2.6 percent over the 12 months ending in December 2023. Core PCE inflation declined to 2.9 percent, the lowest reading since early 2021. Goods price inflation has returned to near zero as supply chains have normalized, and the disinflationary impulse is now increasingly dependent on continued moderation in housing and services costs. The labor market has remained resilient, with the unemployment rate near historically low levels and wage growth moderating gradually. Real GDP growth was solid in 2023 despite the substantial tightening of monetary policy, surprising many forecasters who had anticipated recession. Market participants have revised down their expectations for the path of the federal funds rate and now anticipate several cuts beginning in 2024, as disinflation continues and the economy approaches a soft landing. The narrative of immaculate disinflation has gained credibility, though the last mile of returning inflation to the 2 percent target may prove more challenging.
